headline: Long-form writing partner — feedback on 8 novel chapters with character-name and worldbuilding consistency tracking

text: Terry. I'm writing a novel — first draft. I want you to help me hold consistency across chapters. Setup: it's a low-fantasy set in a port city called Asher's Reach. Three POVs.

POV 1 — Tess, a 28-year-old harbor inspector with a limp from a childhood accident. POV 2 — her younger brother Ren, a thief working for the Greyport Syndicate. POV 3 — Magistrate Velos, the city's chief lawkeeper, ambitious and lonely.
Save those three as character entity-pages. Audience terry only. Tag 'novel:asher-reach'.
Now Chapter 1 opening — Tess inspects a smuggling ship. She finds a crate of small, identical wax-sealed jars. Help me write the opening paragraph. Tense, present, close 3rd person.
OK try again — too florid. Strip it to bones. The wax seals matter, the jars matter, her limp matters but show don't tell.
Better. Save that as Chapter 1 opening to a vault note. Tag 'novel:asher-reach' and 'chapter-1'.
Now — Ren's introduction in Chapter 2. He's running across rooftops at night. What's his voice — what makes him different from Tess?
Right, more impulsive, more present-moment. Tess plans, Ren reacts. Save that as a craft note for the project.
Draft Ren's first paragraph. Same tense and POV close-3rd, but with his voice.
Save Chapter 2 opening. Tag accordingly.
Chapter 3 — Velos. Magistrate's office, late night, reading reports about smuggling. He's older, formal, carries the weight of the office. Draft the opening.
Save Chapter 3 opening.
Now I want to check consistency. Pull the three chapter openings. Are the three voices distinct enough?
Be specific. What makes Tess sound like Tess and not like Velos? I need diagnostics, not 'they're different'.
Right. Save that as a craft note — 'voice diagnostics'. We'll come back to it.
Chapter 4 — Tess again. She follows the smuggling lead and finds Ren in the warehouse. They haven't seen each other in 3 years. Draft the moment they recognize each other.
What was Ren's relationship to Tess? Pull from his entity page.
Right — she raised him after their parents died. Make sure that history is in the recognition scene without infodumping.
Save Chapter 4.
Chapter 5 — Velos summons Tess. Bureaucratic dialogue, but he's noticing her competence in a way that's not entirely professional. Draft.
Hmm — Velos shouldn't flirt. He's lonely, not predatory. Try again with that distinction.
Better. Save Chapter 5.
Chapter 6 — Ren on the run. The Syndicate is hunting him because Tess found the jars. Draft a tight chase scene.
Wait — what was in the jars again? I never said. We've been writing around it for 5 chapters.
Pull every chapter and find the references to the jars. List them so I can decide what they should be.
Right. They could be: drug, magical reagent, blackmail letters, plague vials, religious relics. Which one buys me the most plot for the book I want to write?
Magical reagent. Specifically — distilled from a plant that only grows on the islands the city used to colonize. So it's drug-shaped but with imperial-history baggage.
Save 'jar contents' as a worldbuilding note. Audience terry only. Tag 'novel:asher-reach' and 'worldbuilding'.
Now — go back to chapters 1-6 and tell me which lines need to change to seed this answer earlier without spoiling it.
Be conservative — change as little as possible. Two or three small touches per chapter.
Save those as a 'revision pass 1' note. We'll do them later, not now.
Chapter 7 — Tess and Velos. He confronts her with what she found. She lies to protect Ren. Draft the dialogue.
What's Tess's tell when she lies? We haven't established one. Make one up — something physical, small, repeats.
She touches her bad leg. Subtle but consistent. Add that to her entity page as a tic.
Save Chapter 7.
Chapter 8 — Ren reaches out to Tess. She has to choose: turn him in or hide him. End of Act 1. Draft the final paragraph — the choice itself, not the explanation.
Save Chapter 8. Now read me back what we have — list all 8 chapters, one-line summaries, in order. I want to see the act shape.
Save that as 'novel structure - act 1' note. Audience terry. We're done for today.
